data.title: China's Gold Reserves Going Through The Roof
data.description:
The People’s Bank of China (PBoC) continues to buy unprecedented amounts of gold as the global financial is deleveraging (i.e. investors exchange credit assets for gold).

In 2024, the Chinese central bank covertly bought 570 tonnes, encouraging gold’s ascent in global international reserves by 4%, the largest gain in four decades.

Gargantuan Gold Purchases by the Chinese Central Bank

This article is an analysis of formal and informal sources that indicate the PBoC is currently sitting on more than 5,000 tonnes of monetary gold located in Beijing – more than TWICE what has been publicly admitted.

Legacy media outlets routinely withhold crucial information from investors by principally reporting on official data on gold purchases by monetary authorities. In reality, the Chinese central bank is buying many multiples of what it’s officially disclosing, and investors should take note.

Combine large purchases by the PBoC and other regional central banks with initiatives in the East to settle trade through non-dollar ventures like mBridge and possibly gold stablecoins, and we should expect the international monetary system to continue shifting towards gold in the years ahead. Especially given today’s excessive debt levels, geopolitical tensions, and trade wars.

China Is Secretively Buying 5x More Gold Than Is Reported

Since the Ukraine war began, data show, China’s central bank has been buying roughly five times more gold than what it discloses to the International Monetary Fund (IMF).

But it’s more difficult to get an accurate estimate of how much it owned at the start of the war so we can determine how much it owns now, in total. More on that later.

Let us start off with data from the World Gold Council’s (WGC) quarterly Gold Demand Trends reports on precious metal buying by central banks in aggregate. From the WGC:

Central bank demand is calculated using information from three different sources. Monthly International Financial Statistics produced by the IMF serve as an initial check for central bank transactions… A second vital source is confidential information regarding unrecorded sales and purchases. The final element in calculated net central bank purchases is analysis of trade flow data.

These confidential sources regarding unrecorded purchases and trade flow data are important themes in our present analysis, as there is no other way to ascertain what the PBoC does behind closed doors.

By comparing the WGC’s quarterly estimates with official data from the IMF, a glaring disparity is revealed since 2022 when the war in Ukraine broke out.

Chart 1. The difference between WGC and IMF data reflects unreported central bank gold buying.

According to two sources familiar with the matter, but who prefer to stay anonymous, the difference is mainly caused by “unreported purchases” by the Chinese central bank.

My approach has always been to take eighty percent of the difference between WGC and IMF figures and label that as secretive Chinese buying.

For instance, using this approach, I calculated that the People's Bank of China acquired 280 tonnes during the third quarter of 2022.

In December 2022, the Financial Times published a similar estimate that confirms the accuracy of my sources and methodology:

Mark Bristow, chief executive of Barrick Gold, the world’s second-largest gold miner, said China had bought tonnes of gold around the high-200s mark [in Q3 2022], based on his discussions with numerous sources.

Gold industry insiders — whether working at bullion banks, mining companies, refineries, consultancy firms, or secure logistics companies — sometimes exchange information. It’s through these interactions that estimates are conceived about the volume of secret gold buying by the Chinese central bank.

Thus, if one has access to one or multiple nodes in this network, it can piggybank on their intelligence. More on this below.

Other evidence of the PBoC’s covert gold acquisitions is the surplus in the Chinese gold market since 2022.

In the past three years, China’s import plus domestic mine supply exceeded withdrawals from the vaults of the Shanghai Gold Exchange (SGE). As the SGE is the center gold bourse in China — through laws and tax incentives, most supply and demand flows through this exchange — an observed surplus must reflect acquisitions by the local monetary authority.

Chart 2. I’ve checked with sources close to the SGE that tell me the giant surplus is not being accumulated in SGE vaults, so it must end up in the PBoC’s vaults.

Another piece of evidence is the gold exported directly from the London Bullion Market to China. Because in London gold trade is conducted in large 400-ounce bars, but on the SGE, the private sector only trades in 1-kilogram bars, any gold moving directly from the U.K. to China is destined for the Chinese central bank.

The smoking gun as to Chinese government stockpiling is when we see a continuation of large exports from Britain to China, whilst gold in Shanghai is trading at a discount versus London.

Obviously, no bullion bank would buy gold in London to sell at a loss in China. What actually happens is that the PBoC buys gold in London and lets bullion banks transport the metal to Beijing. In these circumstances, bullion banks (not the PBoC) must deal with customs departments and so the flow of gold is recorded.

Chart 3. Gold exports of 400-ounce bars from the U.K. to China go straight to the PBoC. China’s central bank has surreptitiously bought 1,800 tonnes from Q3 2022 through the end of 2024, which is five times more than what’s officially reported.

The PBoC Secretly Holds 5,000+ Tonnes of Gold

For dessert, let’s estimate how much gold the Chinese central bank owns in total.

We know how much gold it bought every quarter going back to 2010 when the WGC’s Gold Demand Trends data began. To get a ballpark estimate of how much it owns now, all we need is an estimate of how much it owned at any point in time since 2010.

I have been fortunate that, on two occasions, well-connected industry insiders shared with me how much they thought the Chinese central bank owns. On the first occasion, in late 2015, an insider told me that from within his network, he tallied 3,300 tonnes. The second source was more conservative and conveyed in June of 2024 his estimate was approximately 4,000 tonnes at that time.

When I reverse engineer the second estimate to 2015, then take the average of the two estimates, and calculate what has been added per quarter ever since, it produces an outcome of 5,065 tonnes for the end of 2024.

So, to the best of my knowledge, 5,065 tonnes is the weight of fine gold that the Chinese central bank currently stores in the capital Beijing.

China Lifts Gold in Global International Reserves

If we take into account the PBoC’s hidden gold reserves, as well as surreptitious additions by the Central Banks of Saudi Arabia and others, world official reserves reached an all-time high of 39,547 tonnes at the end of 2024.

As virtually all new monetary gold buyers are located in the East, non-Western gold holdings are rising rapidly and have reached 45% of world gold reserves. The following chart is a perfect visual representation of the shift in global power towards the East.

Chart 4. Non-Western central banks nearly hold as much gold as their Western counterparts.

My personal estimates of world official gold reserves, combined with Currency Composition of Official Foreign Exchange Reserves (COFER) data by the IMF, unveil gold’s percentage of global international reserves jumped up 4%, to 21%, in 2024. That’s the biggest surge in more than four decades.

Chart 5. Next to gold, small currencies (“other”) are also on the rise in international reserves.

Next time you read claims that there is no de-dollarization actually occurring, please urge the naive writer to include gold in his statistics as to foreign reserves!

Notes

To properly understand why gold exports from the U.K. to China must be for the PBoC, please refer to my previous articles (here, here, and here) that take into account all the mechanics of the Chinese domestic gold market.

For more information on why PBoC gold purchases are visible in customs data, read the following comments.

Due to the second new source from 2024, I have revised my estimate downwards from previous publications.

Original Post

data.title: WiseTech Global: A Mix Of Positives And Negatives
data.description:
I still have a mixed opinion of WiseTech Global (OTCPK:WIGBY) (OTCPK:WTCHF) (WTC.AU) as a potential investment candidate.

My December 10, 2024 initiation piece highlighted the divergence in WTC's short- and long-term outlooks.

WTCHF continues to warrant a Hold rating in view of recent market and corporate developments.

Stock Sentiment Could Be Hurt By Trade Turbulence And Leadership Transition

WIGBY is making changes to its board and management at a time when freight prospects have turned negative.

It's understandable why institutional investor "AustralianSuper" divested its full 2% stake in the company last month. There could be further shareholder churn in the near future, if people are uncomfortable with the latter's economic and governance risks.

The worldwide container shipping volume witnessed a -2 percent contraction in February 2025 on year-on-year terms, in contrast with a +5% expansion in the prior three-month term. These numbers are taken from UBS Group's (UBS) recent March 26 report (not available publicly) titled "Software Tracker" which details alternative data metrics for Australian technology businesses.

This same research note published by the European bank indicated that WTCHF's number of recruitment advertisements decreased by -11% YoY at the beginning of Mar-25. It's reasonable to think that the company has adopted a more prudent stance towards talent investment.

New tariffs disclosed by Trump on April 2 bring greater uncertainty to the global economy. 3PL ("third-party-logistics") companies, WIGBY's main clients, will most probably cut back on IT expenditures in this environment. As a provider of logistics-related software, WTC's near-term revenue growth could be under pressure.

Separately, there are questions raised about its directors and CEO.

At the end of the prior month, WTCHF disclosed that it has brought in two new independent board members, including "Andrew Harrison" or "AH". Notably, Harrison was previously the company's chairman between Sep-2018 and Mar-2024. One of WIGBY's shareholders, an Australian fund called "HESTA", wasn't convinced of AH's independence for this reason according to a local media article.

Also, there hasn't been a permanent CEO in place for the past six months.

WIGBY's current interim chief executive is its CFO "Andrew Cartledge" or "AC". His expertise and experience are mainly in financial matters.

Investors might prefer someone else with a background in logistics or software to be at the helm. It's hard to predict who will be the new CEO, and when he or she will come on board.

I think that a valuation premium for WiseTech isn't justified now. This takes into consideration external headwinds and concerns about the company's future decision-makers.

But Company Is Gaining Share And Becoming More Profitable

I do like WTC's improving competitive position and its efforts to transform into a leaner entity, notwithstanding specific negatives mentioned above.

WTCHF's goal is to become the software of choice for as many Freight Forwarders ("FFs") as possible.

It reached a critical milestone in the earlier part of this year. Over half of the top-25 FFs in the world have already inked contracts with WIGBY. This was made possible with two new Japanese customer additions in 3Q24 ("LOGISTEED") and 1Q25 ("Nippon Express") (calendar year terms), respectively. Its market share is increasing on the back of recent wins.

The best thing is that WTCHF's positive client momentum isn't reflected in its results to a large degree yet. Only a fifth of the aggregate potential user base for WIGBY's FF customers are currently "signed-up and active" on the company's platform thus far.

There's a robust backlog of contracted topline that can be realized over time to support its future financial performance.

Also, WTC's operating profitability is getting better.

The EBITDA-to-sales metric for WIGBY grew by roughly +500 bps ("basis points") to 50% in 1HFY25 on a YoY basis.

My prediction is that the company's EBITDA margin can widen by +4 ppts ("percentage points") to 51 percent for full-year FY25. This will be driven by both lower expenses and higher ASPs ("Average Selling Prices").

I drew attention to a slowdown in WTC's hiring activity in the previous section. While this has unfavorable read-throughs for the revenue outlook, it could boost WTCHF's bottomline. Two months ago, WIGBY raised its business restructuring-related cost savings guidance for the current fiscal year by a quarter to $25M.

WTCHF has a powerful margin expansion lever. It has more than a thousand enhancements planned for its existing software solutions in the current year. This gives the company a good reason to implement price hikes.

My Conclusion

The market is currently valuing WTC at 20 times (source: S&P Capital IQ) next twelve months' Enterprise Value-to-Revenue ("EV/R"), which is reasonable. I stay Neutral on this name.

On a historical basis, the stock's all-time average EV/R multiple was pretty close at 19x.

WTCHF also boasts a growth-adjusted EV-to-sales ratio of 0.91 ("20/22"), considering the company's consensus FY24-29 topline CAGR of +22%. This is close to 1x indicative of fair valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chipotle: Pain Is Not Over Yet
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Chipotle (CMG) in November, I rated the stock a “hold” with a price target of $56.5, as I believed that it did not provide an attractive upside, given the weakening demand environment.

Since then, the stock has underperformed the S&P 500, declining over 11% during this period of time.

Chipotle reported its Q4 FY24 earnings in February, where it saw its revenue and adjusted earnings per share grow 13.1% and 19% to $2.8B and $0.25, respectively. While the management discussed their strategies to expand throughout by investing in innovative back-of-house technologies, coupled with a strong marketing plan that is centered on promoting healthy eating habits, there is no denying that the company will face tougher comps, where the management projected their comparable sales to grow in the low to mid-single digit range.

In the meantime, Chipotle's margins are under pressure and could face another 60 basis points of contraction should Trump’s recently announced tariffs go into full effect, as per management commentary during the Q4 earnings call.

The company is due to report its Q1 FY25 earnings on April 23, where investors will be paying close attention to the management’s forward revision to its top line as well as specific commentary on how they plan to navigate the tariff headwinds to protect their bottom lines.

Given the unknown nature of how far the derating can extend, I believe the short-term pain is not over yet. As a result, I will reiterate my “hold” rating with a price target of $55.

Improving throughput coupled with a strong marketing plan to deliver single-to-mid digit comp growth in FY25

Chipotle reported their Q4 FY24 earnings, where revenue grew 13.1% YoY to $2.8B, remaining unchanged on a quarter-over-quarter basis. During the quarter, Chipotle saw its comparable sales grow at 5.4%, slower than the growth rate of its previous quarter at 6%, driven by 4.0% and 1.4% growth in transaction volume and average check size, respectively. Plus, the company also managed to open 119 new company-owned restaurants during the quarter, 80% of which have a Chipotlane, bringing their total restaurant count to 3437, up 7.8% YoY in FY24.

Moving forward, the management expects to face tougher comps, where they have projected for their comparable restaurant sales to grow in the low to mid-single-digit range for FY25, as opposed to 7.4% YoY growth in comparable sales in FY24.

However, the management believes that their incremental throughput opportunity and strong marketing plan should enable them to deliver positive transaction comps growth in FY25, despite facing macro challenges.

During the earnings call, the management outlined the importance of an experienced general manager who understands the importance of throughput and can train the crew accordingly. On that note, the management noted that their GM turnover continued to improve in 2024 and is at the lowest level in the company’s history. This is reflected in improving throughput by about two entrées in the peak 15-minute period in 2024, which I believe indicates growing stability in restaurant and guest experiences.

Looking forward, the management is doubling down on modernizing the back of house to increase the efficiency and simplicity of preparation in order to further improve their throughput. For instance, they are currently in the middle of rolling out their produce slicers to all restaurants and expect the rollout to be complete by summer, enabling restaurants to save time on one of the most time-consuming tasks while maintaining their high culinary standards. Plus, Chipotle has also begun a new initiative in selected restaurants of installing their produce slicer, dual-sided plancha, three-pan rice cooker, and dual-vat fryer to estimate the magnitude of throughput improvement. Based on initial results, the management has decided to roll out all four pieces of equipment to new restaurant openings in FY25, aimed at “making tasks easier to execute, more efficient, faster, and more consistent while maintaining their high culinary standards.”

In addition to expanding throughput through innovative back-of-house technologies to improve guest experience, the management is prioritizing their marketing strategy to make the Chipotle brand more visible and relevant, which also plays a key role in driving both transaction volume and order sizes. In fact, their marketing strategy for 2025 is centered on lifestyle goals to promote healthy eating habits among their guests in North America and Europe, where their messaging displays their high-quality, responsibly sourced ingredients that can be customized to fit any dietary restrictions or lifestyle routines, with exciting menu innovations that include their Chipotle Honey Chicken Pilot coming to restaurants soon.

International markets and Chipotlane as potential growth levers

One more area where Chipotle is doubling down is its international markets, which, I believe, can act as a revenue diversifier.

At the end of FY24, Chipotle had 85 international restaurants, with 55 in Canada, 27 in Europe, and 3 in the Middle East. Currently, the revenue contribution from its international markets is still miniscule at roughly 1.8% of Total Revenue.

However, the management discussed that Chipotle is resonating across geographies and that they plan to accelerate growth in Canada under new leadership and in the Middle East in partnership with Alshaya Group in FY25.

I also wanted to touch in on Chipotle’s Chipotlane strategy, as they surpassed their 1000th Chipotle lane, which is a drive-through format that makes up over 25% of their restaurants. As they plan on building Chipotlanes in 80% of all their new restaurant openings, the management reiterated that an average Chipotle lane takes less than 30 seconds to complete the order pickup process and continues to generate better revenues and margins compared to restaurants that do not have a Chipotlane.

Margins remain under pressure along with tariff uncertainty.

Shifting gears to profitability, Chipotle saw its margins come under pressure in Q4. Starting with its cost of sales, which includes Food, Beverage, and Packaging costs, it amounted to 30.4% of Total Revenue, up 50 basis points on a year-over-year basis but 20 basis points lower than the previous quarter. The year-over-year increase in cost of sales was driven by a higher usage of ingredients as they focused on generous portions, along with a protein mix shift to their Smoked Brisket limited-time offer.

In terms of their operating costs, labor costs amounted to 25.2% of total revenue, an increase of 20 basis points from the previous year and 30 basis points from the previous quarter as a result of minimum wage increases at all their restaurant locations in California.

Despite a higher cost of sales and labor as a percentage of revenue, Chipotle still managed to grow its operating income over 14% YoY to $415.7M, with a margin expansion of 20 basis points to 14.6%, driven by sales leverage and a lower delivery mix. In the meantime, their diluted earnings per share grew 20% YoY to $0.24, in line with expectations.

Moving forward, there is admittedly a huge uncertainty pertaining to the potential impact of tariffs on margins. During the earnings call, the management discussed that they source 2% of their sales from Mexico, which includes avocados, tomatoes, limes, and peppers, and less than 0.5% from Canada and China. Plus, they mentioned that they might see an impact of about 60 basis points on their cost of sales should Trump’s recently announced tariffs go into full effect.

Yesterday, President Trump imposed a worldwide baseline tariff rate of 10%, which will go into effect on April 5, with additional tariffs that will be added for countries that the administration considers to be the worst offenders. With Evercore ISI estimating that the new weighted-average tariff rate could jump to 29% once all new duties are enacted, it will mark the highest level in more than 100 years.

In all of this, Chipotle’s management has reiterated their focus on supply chain efficiencies and equipment upgrades to mitigate tariff-related costs. However, the question remains as to whether the margin headwinds are fully priced in forward analyst estimates, which has seen a storm of negative downward revisions over the last three months, as can be seen below.

What to watch out for in Q1 FY25?

Chipotle is due to report its Q1 FY25 earnings on April 23, where revenue is expected to grow 10% YoY to $2.97B during the quarter. While meeting or exceeding its revenue targets will be helpful, investors will be looking for forward revisions to its revenue guidance. Should the management continue to demonstrate resilience through a combination of comparable sales growth in its domestic markets and momentum in its international markets, investors may feel a sense of renewed optimism.

In the meantime, Chipotle is expected to generate $0.28 in adjusted diluted earnings per share, which will mark a growth of just 3.7%. On the earnings front, investors will be looking for specific management commentary on the impact of tariffs on the company’s cost of sales and overall margin profile.

Should the management’s estimates come at or above investors’ expectations, we may see a bottoming in forward earnings revisions, which could also help provide a floor for the stock price. In the alternative case, analysts will derate the stock further, downgrading their forward estimates for both revenue and earnings for FY25 and beyond.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will take the forward consensus estimates for revenue growth, where Chipotle grows at a slower pace in FY25 at 11.8%, followed by a revenue acceleration in the 13% range in the following years, translating to a total revenue of $16.3B by FY27. This will be driven by a combination of growth in comparable sales and new restaurant openings in both its domestic and international markets, as the company makes progress towards its long-term vision of 7,000 restaurants in North America with AUV (Average Unit Volume) surpassing $4M.

From a profitability standpoint, analysts are projecting for adjusted earnings per share to grow 15% in FY25, before accelerating in the high teens range in the following years, especially as the company continues to improve its unit economics through innovative technologies to expand throughput and sales leverage. This will translate to an adjusted earnings per share of $1.84 by FY27, which will be equivalent to a present value of $1.46 when discounted at 8%.

Taking the S&P 500 as a proxy, where its companies grow their earnings on average by 8% every year over a 10-year period, I believe Chipotle should trade at least at 2.25 times the price-to-earnings ratio, which translates to a PE ratio of 38, or a price target of $55, representing an upside of 6.7% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

The way I see it, the short-term pain may not yet be over. At least, not until Q1 FY25, where we will get more specific insight into how the company intends to manage tariff-related headwinds to protect its top and bottom lines.

I would like to note, however, that on a longer-term basis, I believe that the management is placing the right strategic bets where it is investing in innovative technologies to improve its throughput per restaurant location while doubling down on its international markets as well as Chipotlanes.

As a result, I believe that beyond FY25, revenue and profitability should be accelerating from what analysts are projecting for the current year.

But, at the moment, given the unknown nature of how far the decaying environment will extend, I will remain cautious and rate the stock a “hold” with a price target of $55.

data.title: Making America's Chip Fabs Great Again
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

As national security threats mount, the U.S. aims to revive production of complex computer chips, creating potential opportunities all along the semi supply chain.

The U.S. used to be the global leader in semiconductor manufacturing. Yet decades of offshoring have left the country heavily reliant on advanced computer chips made in Taiwan, Japan and South Korea. To put the current imbalance in perspective: Taiwan’s TSMC (TSM), the world’s largest chipmaker, now produces more than half of the world’s advanced chips.1

While it’s true that U.S. juggernauts such as Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM) and Broadcom (AVGO) have revolutionized everything from wireless communication to artificial intelligence, and that members of the Magnificent 7 (including Apple, Amazon and Tesla) now design their own chips, none of these companies make the little engines chugging at the core of their products. There would be no Apple iPhones or Tesla cars without Taiwan’s chip manufacturing ecosystem.

Yet two recent developments may be marking a crucial turning point for the U.S. semiconductor industry, potentially unlocking attractive opportunities for selective investors all along the semi supply chain.

In March 2025, TSMC announced a fresh $100 billion investment to build various chip facilities in the U.S. — the largest single foreign direct investment in U.S. history. Specifically, the Taiwanese giant plans to establish five plants, including three fabs, two packaging facilities, and a research and development center.2

Also in March, Intel (INTC), which has lost ground to competitors in recent years, appointed industry veteran Lip-Bu Tan as its new CEO. Renowned for his successful turnaround of chip design software firm Cadence (CDNS) and his impact on the Electronic Design Automation (EDA) industry over the past decade, Tan is expected to prioritize expanding Intel’s foundry services and advancing AI chip production in seeking to regain the firm’s formerly formidable manufacturing prowess.3

We believe these moves — and perhaps more to come — could signal a broader revival across the U.S. semiconductor ecosystem, creating ripple effects across the economy and unlocking compelling opportunities for investors. The expansion of U.S.-based fabs is expected to drive significant growth in sectors critical to chip production, including equipment manufacturing (think complex lithography machines and wafer-handling equipment), construction and specialty chemicals, among other areas.

Both the Biden and Trump administrations have emphasized the strategic importance of semiconductors, framing the revival of domestic chip manufacturing as both a technological imperative and a matter of economic security. According to the Semiconductor Industry Association (SIA), U.S. fab capacity is projected to grow by an impressive 203% between 2022 and 2032.4

For investors, we believe this resurgence arrives at an opportune time, as semiconductor stock valuations have retreated in the wake of DeepSeek’s announcement that it had developed a large language AI model requiring far less computing power (i.e. fancy chips). With demand for critical production inputs set to accelerate, we believe companies at the forefront of a potential U.S. chipmaking renaissance are strategically positioned to capitalize on emerging opportunities.

Footnotes

1 “Taiwan’s dominance of the chip industry makes it more important,” The Economist, March 6, 2023;

2 “Trump and TSMC announce $100 billion plan to build five new U.S. factories”, Reuters, March 4, 2025;

3 “Exclusive: Intel’s new CEO plots overhaul of manufacturing and rel="noopener noreferrer" AI operations,” Reuters, March 17, 2025;

4“Strengthening the U.S. Semiconductor Supply Chain,” SIA, 2024.

data.title: Qualcomm And The Explosion Of Edge Computing
data.description:
Thesis

Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) is one of the names I’ve followed for the longest time – nearly three decades. Back in the 1990s Investor’s Business Daily (an up & coming newspaper) highlighted the trials & tribulations of the “Holy War of Wireless Standards” to which Qualcomm’s CDMA protocol eventually won. Since then, Qualcomm’s power rose steadily as the U.S. cellular phone penetration-rate rose from the low single digits to 100%. Qualcomm’s potential ebbed as wireless matured. Going forward, I see a new market in IoT (Internet of Things) that will leverage Qualcomm’s core competencies and expand its TAM.

Introduction:

Qualcomm is an important enabler within the wireless industry. It designs semiconductors, software & services focused around wireless technology. The company has one of the largest patent portfolios in the world. It operates under two business segments: QCT (semiconductors) QTL (technology licensing). This article is geared towards QCOM’s potential to capture businesses within exponential technologies and will not focus on recent results.

Semiconductor Industry

The chip industry can best be described as growth-cyclical. There is a constant expansion (13% CAGR) of TAM ($697Bn) due to expanding use-cases. The industry has shifted from being a vertically integrated one to “fabless” where companies outsource production. Companies, thus, have become more specialized (Micron: memory, Nvidia: GPU, Intel: CPU) and some companies (e.g., ARM) focus on IP licensing.

Internet of Things (IoT) market

IoT wirelessly connects all kinds of devices to each other, to you and to the Internet via Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, etc. Honestly, when I first heard of IoT, I considered it yet another “shiny object” the industry was promoting. I once questioned, “Do we REALLY need our toaster to be connected ?!” We’ve come a long way since and the benefits of IoT are growing daily. IoT allows for remote control and smart assistants, so homeowners could control their lights, thermostats and appliances. It helps lower electricity prices by reducing heating and shutting lights when you’re not home.

Outside of the home, wearables can help track vitals and promote healthier living. And the list goes on. What particularly interested me is that Moore’s Law is constantly improving semiconductor chips and services, allowing IoT to grow in importance. Let’s not forget that in 2023, Nvidia (NVDA) had its “iPhone Moment” which transformed traditional computer intelligence into AI and neural networks. If what I’ve just said doesn’t convince you of the merits of IoT, then consider that AI Agents will again transform IoT, allowing for real-time processing, seamless communication and context awareness. For example, an AI Agent can schedule maintenance on an HVAC system based on its usage data.

Total Addressable Market (TAM)

At Qualcomm’s 2024 Investor Day, management gave a series of presentations I encourage you to read. Management estimates the total “Edge-computing” TAM at $900Bn by 2030. While Qualcomm was clearly “talking their book” I’m confident this TAM could be reached given what other companies (Nvidia, Tesla, Microsoft) are doing in the space.

Automotive Market

I’m particularly impressed with Qualcomm’s automotive business, but its revenue scale (< $3.3Bn TTM) doesn’t tell the whole story.

They’ve managed to “land and expand” in what I consider the most lucrative areas: infotainment systems and autonomous. QCOM’s automotive pipeline is nearly $50Bn and if they recognized that revenue, it would easily become its largest end market. Their autonomous auto-stack was co-developed with BMW (OTC:BMWKY) and will commercialize next year, allowing Qualcomm to recognize significant revenues on its financial statements. Qualcomm management’s targeting the Auto segment to grow from $3Bn to $8Bn annually by FY ’29. The “QCT IoT” business plus “Auto” together is targeted at $22Bn in FY ’29, which would be over 50% of Qualcomm’s total FY ’24 revenues!

Beyond 2029

Qualcomm’s XR platform offers the most innovative and exciting prospects. While it’s a small business segment for Qualcomm (just $2Bn FY ’29 targeted), I see great optionality as it leverages QCOM’s core competencies and offers a “blue-ocean” of demand. Readers need to experience (as I have) Virtual Reality, Augmented Reality and Mixed Reality in what feels like a Sci-fi movie.

Revenue Growth and Balance Sheet

I won’t go into details on Qualcomm’s financial ratios as the company has been well stewarded since its IPO. For example, it pays a dividend in a responsible manner and while it has debt, its Net Debt (after cash equivalents) is zero. Qualcomm has had somewhat volatile revenue growth – surprising considering the somewhat predictable global handset market. Its efforts to diversify into automotive and IoT markets should lessen this volatility and lift valuation multiples.

Recent Results & Outlook

Qualcomm’s 1Q ’25 earnings results were good overall as results beat estimates. However, QCOM’s shares declined after-hours on lowered guidance. For additional information, I encourage you to read SA author Daniel Schönberger’s earnings analysis.

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG)

Qualcomm ranks highly for ESG. New (2018) CEO Cristiano Amon has been instrumental in shaping Qualcomm’s direction towards new initiatives. The CEO appointment boosted its corporate governance as the Jacobs family (which founded the company in 1985) no longer has direct influence. (Note: “G” in the table means Qualcomm hasn’t been involved in any major controversies.)

Risks:

Given that the wireless services industry is an oligopolistic one, Qualcomm benefits from having a few large, creditworthy customers. However, the flip-side is the potential for revenue volatility, etc. (risks are below).

  • Revenue volatility (e.g., Large loss in Apple’s chipset business (see pie-chart))
  • Smartphone market becoming mature
  • Tariffs and trade war
  • IoT is an evolving market with new competitors

I believe that Qualcomm’s lost Apple business, of which will be 80% gone by 2027, is a medium-term headwind, but it’s not a “death-blow” as Qualcomm has known about this for two years, and it’s been forced to enter new markets. Frankly, I believe when we look back on this, we’ll view it as an opportunity. As Winston Churchill said, “Never let a good crises go to waste.”

Valuation

The Apple customer-loss, worries over an “AI indigestion” phase, and the Trade War have pushed down QCOM’s share price. The table below shows different EV/forward Revenue scenarios and what QCOM’s shares would be worth for each (blue bar). QCOM trades at 3.9x EV/Rev as of this writing. While it could conceptionally trade at 2.5x (similar to Intel), I believe that is a low probability unless we’re in a tighter monetary situation as was the case in 10/2022 (see chart).

Alternatively, it’s doubtful QCOM can trade at 7.8x Revenue (12/2020) or at 29.6x (ARM Holdings) as 2020-21 was a period of excess monetary liquidity and Qualcomm isn’t a pure-licensing company like ARM.

An EV/Revenue ratio of 6x seems achievable (even considering slowing growth). This translates to a stock price of $235/share, or 55% greater than today’s price.

Conclusion:

As many readers know, I follow the blockchain/technology space closely. We’re witnessing a movement from centralization & control (e.g., Amazon, Google) to decentralization – both in “Web2” and blockchain. It isn’t just control of our private-data, but processing power is moving from a centralized to decentralized one.

Even in the highly centralized hyperscaler space, we’re witnessing decentralization as AI models shift from training on horizontal LLM (Large Language Models) to vertical [SLM] models. Concurrently, processing is moving from training at huge data-centers to inference at the edge (smartphones, autos, robots, appliances, etc.)

When one considers how well-suited Qualcomm is to the Exponential Age, it becomes obvious that Qualcomm shares are a “Strong Buy.”

data.title: Exxon Mobil Corporation: Bullish Momentum Should Be Fueled Up
data.description:
Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) maintains a decent topline performance amid energy commodity price volatility. It perfectly understands market dynamics and how it can balance volume and margins. Despite market uncertainties, XOM remains well-positioned with its prudent business portfolio diversification and strategic inventory management. In addition, its solid balance sheet shows it can cover its large operating capacity, borrowings, and dividends. These can support my bullish view of this stock, as it appears a bit underpriced.

XOM’s Q4 2024: Challenged But Solid

Exxon Mobil Corporation has faced various challenges in recent years amid global economic and political uncertainties. Yet, it continues to exude resilience and maintain a decent performance and strategic market positioning. These are reflected by its recent performances. Its operating revenue amounted to $83.4B, down by -1.1% YoY from $84.3B. Various aspects contributed to it.

First, oil price volatility remained a challenge for the company. As we can see, WTI Crude Oil price dropped from its peak price of $86-87 per barrel to $70-71 at the end of the year. Meanwhile, the price of Brent plunged from $91-92 to $74-76 per barrel. The increased output of non-OPEC producers like the US put downward pressure on prices. This mainly affected XOM’s upstream segment. Note that XOM derives a substantial portion of its revenues from its oil and gas production. As we can see, its crude oil and bitumen net production volume reached 3.2M barrels per day versus 2.6M in Q4 2023.

Another factor was the sticky inflation that further softened spending and consumption across various industries. We also saw a -64% monthly import drop in October as more products were rerouted to inventory restocking. The massive rebound in the US Dollar strength measure in DXY was also a factor, making US-based oil and gas more expensive for other countries. All these things contributed to a lower demand for its refined products, such as gasoline and diesel.

Despite this, natural gas experienced a sustained strength, which partially offset the weakness in its oil business. In addition, XOM maintained prudent crude oil inventory management and scaled back its exploration activities and production to stabilize costs and margins. As we can see, its net oil upstream volumes rose by 17% or over 400,000 barrels per day from Q4 2023 to Q2 2024. In my view, that was logical considering the increasing crude oil prices and stable DXY. But in the second half, its upstream volume increase was slowed to 7.6%.

This proved effective as its net income margin remained flat at 9.5%. In addition, it lowered its cost sensitivity as the operating leverage decreased from 76.3% to 74.4%. The lower variable cost contribution makes XOM more resilient to inflation.

Challenges

Amid its stable topline performance, XOM still faces some challenges, such as the following.

OPEC Output Hike To Affect Oil Prices… Some More

OPEC expressed its intent to raise its output in the next two months by 138,000 bpd and 135,000 bpd, respectively. This can influence global oil prices, which may prompt non-OPEC countries like the US to lower their prices. The impact will be evident in Brent and WTI. They initially rebounded by 13% in anticipation of President Trump’s inauguration last January. Yet, this was short-lived as global uncertainties persist.

Considering OPEC’s move, their prices may either stay within its current range or decrease further. Note that their prices remain near their 52-week lows. So, XOM may experience a weaker pricing flexibility to remain competitive.

On the flip side, its effect in the long run can still benefit the company. Lower oil prices can temper inflation, resume policy easing, and encourage consumption across industries. These can bolster economic activities and increase the demand for its oil and gas, as well as its refined products.

Strong DXY

The DXY is still in a solid upward momentum and considering the economic condition of the US relative to other countries, the trend is likely to continue this year. The Fed tempered inflation after temporarily pausing its rate cuts. Additionally, its GDP growth and unemployment rate are starting to normalize and return to pre-pandemic levels.

While XOM can anticipate an increased domestic demand, a strong DXY can still affect its sales in other countries. This will be more evident in those with weaker currencies against the USD.

Mitigants and Drivers

These are the things I believe can help XOM cushion those risks and even drive its rebound.

Strong Balance Sheet and Cash Flows

Despite its high capital intensity, XOM boasts impressive liquidity and sustainability with its strong cash flows. Its free cash flow or FCF reached $8.0B versus $7.97 in Q4 2023. This means that XOM generated higher cash returns despite a weaker market condition. So, XOM was able to convert nearly 10% of its revenues into free cash. If we exclude the impact of working capital, the value will be $9.5B.

Looking into its Balance Sheet, its cash, inventory, and debt levels remain relatively stable. This also proves how prudent business portfolio diversification and inventory management worked. By knowing what and how much to produce relative to the market, XOM managed its inventory very well. As a result, it avoided unnecessary cash burns and/or financial leverage.

Moreover, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains very low at 0.28x. This only shows that XOM has enough cash reserves and earnings to pay all its borrowings within a quarter of a year. In addition, 88% of its borrowings will not mature within this year. To be more specific, 34% of all borrowings have maturities of more than a decade. So, XOM has a lot of time to cover its borrowings, and a portion of its cash inflows can sustain dividends.

Natural Gas Demand Surge

I already talked a lot about its oil and haven’t even touched natural gas a bit. So, I am reserving this specific section for natural gas. I believe this will be one of the key drivers of XOM despite the increasing calls from government agencies for renewable energies.

Natural Gas is also Exxon Mobil’s cornerstone. Last Q4 2024, it produced 8,331 million cubic feet per day, a 9.0% YoY increase. That is warranted due to the upsurge in demand and price. Currently, it costs $3.98, more than twice its price a year ago. I believe the upsurge will continue considering the increasing demand from the power sector and LNG shipping capacity. Even the US EIA affirms this as it expects natural gas to stabilize at $3.80 before rebounding to $4.20, driven by the anticipated increase in LNG exports. Likewise, the International Energy Agency expects the demand-supply imbalance or shortage to drive natural gas prices further this year. One of the reasons is the increasing global gas demand by 2.8% or 115B cbm in 2024. Another reason is the geopolitical tension in Eastern Europe.

Natural Gas and Data Centers

I believe the demand surge for natural gas is not just a spur of the moment. Although natural gas is not a clean or renewable energy, it can be a transitional fuel source due to lower CO2 emissions.

What I believe will drive its demand and price is the global expansion of data centers. At this point, data centers use 3% of the global electricity supply. This is larger than the total consumption in the UK. This year, Deloitte anticipates the consumption to remain above 500 TWh.

Electricity consumption may increase further as billions or trillions of data center investments enter amid the AI peak. Amazon (AMZN) is a prime example as it intends to allot $100B investment in data centers amid the -ecommerce boom. In addition, the total count of data centers is expected to increase to 6,111 this 2025 and 8,378 in 2030.

In turn, data centers may require an additional 14 GW, equivalent to 2.22 Billion cubic feet per day or Bcf/d in the succeeding years. By 2050, the total demand for natural gas may increase to 24.1 Bcf/d. We must note that these numbers are solely for data centers.

As data centers increase electricity consumption, natural gas demand-supply equilibrium must be maintained. This can push its price up, leading to more XOM revenues. Also, these can have some spillovers on the oil business, since data centers may also require oil-powered generators to ensure continuity even if a grid failure takes place.

Price Ratio

XOM has increased dramatically in recent years. And I think that’s logical if we relate the stock price to its cash levels, expressed as cash per share. Currently, XOM trades at about 22x its cash. While it appears high, it remains cheap relative to the previous years with an average of 30x. If we use this Price/Cash Per Share Ratio to determine XOM’s fair value, the TP will be $160.45, a 34% upside potential.

To prove its logic, I also did a regression analysis. Their 79% correlation shows that cash per share has a substantial impact on price changes. Also, the p-value of 0.0064 confirms the reliability of their relationship. It means that only 0.64% of their direct correlation happened by chance, erasing the bias and randomness of the data. Although these are historical values, their high correlation can help us predict price trends, especially every time the company releases quarterly reports.

DCF Model

For a more forward-looking valuation, I also used the DCF Model. Like the P/CPS Ratio, the model also suggests that XOM is still cheap today. The TP of $140.88 suggests an 18% upside potential.

I assumed its perpetual growth rate at 2.00%. I considered the 2.0% target inflation of the Fed. Also, 2% is conservative and near the current inflation. After all, the performance of its oil segment is heavily impacted by macroeconomic indicators.

Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. I set its risk premium at 5% to cover the risk I will take should I invest here.

Technicals

XOM seems to be rebounding from its recent correction. As we can see, it just exceeded the 200-day SMA, showing a stronger momentum today. Also, the 50-day SMA confirms it as it starts to pick up momentum and comes closer to the 200-day SMA. This tells us that recent positive sentiments continue to push the price upward. Once the 50-day SMA exceeds the 200-day SMA, the bullish trend may continue.

MACD and RSI confirm our bullish view. In fact, early bullish reversal patterns were already detected last March 5. After the correction, the price consolidated before hitting a low of $105.44 and breached the previous low of $105.51. However, the stock’s momentum has already increased substantially, as shown by MACD and RSI. Their low points last March 5 were much higher than on December 20. True enough, the price rebounded. Currently, the price remains bullish. Although there are some bearish rejections, buying volumes still outpace them.

Lastly, note that buying volumes appear to be increasing, contrary to the decreasing selling volume. This tells us that more bulls are accepting the current price as a good entry point.

Conclusion

Exxon Mobil Corporation remains a leading figure in oil and natural gas exploration and production. There are visible headwinds today, but its operational resilience through strategic management and solid Balance Sheet can help it cushion those. Lots of unexpected market opportunities are coming to support its rebound and expansion. In addition, it remains underpriced with attractive upside potential and increasing momentum. I am giving this stock a strong buy rating.

data.title: What's Driving Investment Grade And Where Might It Be Headed?
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit | Todd Schomberg, Senior Portfolio Manager | Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

Investment grade bonds are up 2.34% this year, with much of its solid performance driven by a rally in intermediate yields.1 There has been a lot going on under the hood. Here’s our insights about the various market drivers year-to-date, and where we think investment grade is headed in the coming months.

Craig: Let’s start with rates since they have been the biggest driver of returns this year. They moved late last year, reflecting uncertainty over what the new Trump administration’s policies could mean for inflation. What’s your view on rates in the coming months?

Matt: The market started the year concerned about inflation driven by animal spirits (emotions) as the Trump administration was sworn in. We had a hot Consumer Price Index (CPI) print in January, but inflation has slowed, and the focus has shifted toward future growth. Markets are concerned about tariffs and their potential negative impact on the economy. There’s also concern around immigration policy, which could be growth-negative. Whether tariffs are inflationary, or just one-time price resets, is debatable, but the Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely need to navigate the price impacts of tariffs at the same time that the US economy may be slowing. Given a slower growth outlook, we’d expect the Fed to look through the inflationary impact of tariffs, allowing rates to go lower.

Craig: How have earnings been for industrials? Is credit expansion continuing to drive growth?

Matt: We've seen strong earnings from industrials this year. Utilities have had a somewhat more mixed performance. But if you look at traditional US industrials, they’ve done well.2 The outlook going forward may be more negative or mixed, however. As earnings reports have come out, a lot of companies have beat expectations, but guided forward expectations down, particularly consumer-related companies.

Corporate balance sheets are still in good shape overall. There has been a fair amount of issuance in the investment grade market and some in high yield, but it's been well received. Some companies are taking on debt to pay down debt coming due from previous years, but they appear to be aimed at not levering up.

Craig: Bonds have been negatively correlated to stocks in recent selloffs, such as January’s DeepSeek-related one. What did bonds do during the March stock correction?

Todd: Bonds performed as expected — they were inversely correlated to stocks in these risk-off events. Whenever we’ve had a selloff this year, interest rates have fallen and bonds have rallied. That’s exactly why they may be beneficial in a mix of assets, in our view, for that inverse correlation. With so much uncertainty, especially around growth, we think this negative correlation will continue throughout the rest of the year. Also, many investors have been yield buyers. Upward corrections in yields have the potential to draw buyers back into the market, so bonds could perform well in different situations.

Craig: Investment grade rating upgrades have outstripped downgrades, but Celanase and Nissan were downgraded to high yield. Do you think it’s symptomatic of broader trends or are they idiosyncratic? Also, are there any changes to your bull and bear cases for credit spreads this year?

Todd: The ratio of investment grade credit upgrades to downgrades has risen sharply since the 2020 COVID-19 crisis, to a record 4.7 in 2024.3 Celanese, a global chemical company, and the carmaker Nissan (OTCPK:NSANF)(OTCPK:NSANY) are two one-off situations, in our view, because the companies were poorly run and the downgrades weren’t related to broader fundamental concerns, such as tariffs.

At the end of the day, we still expect more upgrades than downgrades going forward. Of course, if tariffs cause economic activity to slow sharply in the second half of the year, fundamentals could be more challenged, but that isn’t our base case.

For spread expectations, our bull case (15%-20% probability) was for a 55 basis point spread on the investment grade index. That was predicated on a high all-in yield of 4.5% or more on the 10-year Treasury, but not exceeding 5%. In other words, our bull case was dependent on being in the sweet spot of the high 4% level in rates, but without the fear of blowing through the high end of the recent trading range, which might cause panic.

We believed that the high end of the range would likely attract yield-based buyers, such as insurance companies, annuities, and pension plans. Now that we’re trading in the low 4% range on the 10-year Treasury, we’re less optimistic about how much tighter spreads can go.

Our bear case was a widening to around a 120 basis points spread on the index, which has a very small probability, in our view. A key tail risk would be a serious policy misstep, for example. But though there is much volatility and noise around policy, we don't believe economic outcomes will be drastically negative.

Craig: We’ve had spirited debates about the consumer lately. It seems like there’s some softness at the lower end of the consumer spectrum, which has yet to show up in the macro numbers. Have you made any portfolio positioning shifts, or are you thinking about any new risks based on divergent consumer strength and behavior?

Matt: We recently did a deep dive on the consumer. The general conclusion was that there are consumer market segments that are getting squeezed, especially on the lower income end. Is the squeeze big enough to have an impact on the trajectory of the economy and potentially widen credit spreads? We don't think so. We expect some economic slowing this year, so we want to be cautious on certain areas of the economy that could be impacted, like subprime auto and credit card loans. The auto companies could also be susceptible, including foreign ones that could be hit by tariffs. So, we’ve been cautious on areas that could be impacted by a pullback from lower-end consumers. But we don't expect the current consumer dynamics to change the direction of gross domestic product (GDP) or the overall economy at this juncture.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg US Credit Index. The index is up 2.34%, Jan. 1, 2025 to March 21, 2025.

  2. Source: According to FactSet, 78% of S&P 500 industrials companies reported earnings that were above market expectations in Q4 2024. Utilities also beat expectations in several cases, but only 48% reported beats versus consensus estimates, so the results were more mixed. FactSet, Earnings Insight as of March 20, 2025.

  3. Source: JPMorgan, JPM Daily Credit Strategy & CDS/CDX am update, Jan. 7, 2025.

Important information

NA4350644

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

The Bloomberg US Credit Index measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, taxable corporate, and government-related bond markets.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Correlation is the degree to which two investments have historically moved in relation to each other.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures the change in consumer prices and is a commonly cited measure of inflation.

A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments, or other debts.

Credit risk is the risk of default on a debt that may arise from a borrower or issuer of bonds failing to make required payments.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified period of time.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Idiosyncratic developments refer to unique events that do not affect an entire market or portfolio.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Spread represents the difference between two values or asset returns.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of March 25, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Glencore: Positive Near-Term Catalysts Ahead
data.description:
After our follow-up analysis of the mining industry with BHP Group (neutral-rated "Long-Standing Preference For Rio Tinto, Neutral Rating Confirmed"), Rio Tinto (buy-rated "Underappreciated Copper Growth"), and Anglo-American (buy-rated "Disposal Ongoing With More Value To Come"), we are back to comment on Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY). Last time, we positively reported the Teck acquisition, and even if we were incorporating downside pressure on the company's valuation based on Glencore coal assets investment, the company's core EBITDA was backed by the Marketing division and copper. Having said that, Glencore's total return was negative (Fig 1), with an underperformance compared to the other diversified miners by approximately 15% over the last three months. This is mainly due to the weak coal (thermal & met) markets with spot free cash flow yield falling to low single digit number (8%). However, the risk-reward is attractive, and there are several potential positive near-term catalysts for Glencore shares to report.

Fig 1

Why are we positive?

  1. Firstly, the company is actively repurchasing approximately 3 million shares per day, equal to an investment of $12 million. According to our analysis, we calculated that they had spent $335 million of the $1 billion buyback (announced with the Fiscal Year 2024 results in mid-February 2025 — Fig 2). Therefore, they are on track to complete the current program by mid-July (ahead of H1 results in early August). This provides upside potential, and the company might intensify its buyback.
  2. Similar to the recent sentiment on iron ore, Glencore's marketing division earnings have been reviewed downwards by Wall Street analysts. The company guided 2025 EBIT at $3 billion, with long-term estimates of $2.2-3.2 billion. This also included elevated interest rates. This is below Glencore's 2022 peak of $6.4 billion, but the company is well-positioned to take advantage of dislocations created by tariffs. Therefore, we see an upside risk to the 2025 outlook. On a positive note, we expect the company to provide an update on Marketing performance with Q1 results.
  3. Regarding commodities, we believe several positive catalysts are ahead, including 1) thermal/met-coal, 2) copper, and 3) cobalt. Related to thermal & met-coal prices, they are positioned to recover based on seasonal demand and lower supply due to recent closures. Cerrejon is the first significant curtailment in seaborne thermal coal, but smaller US producers have filed for Chapter 11. On the copper, we are positive in the long term. However, the physical copper market is tight, with Chinese demand accelerating. Lower treatment charges have also impacted refined industrial production. So, Glencore will likely benefit from short-term and long-term upside risks. On the cobalt, the price has lifted over 50% over the last four weeks, from ~$10/lb to approximately $15.5/lb. This was driven by the 4-month export ban introduced by the Democratic Republic of Congo government in February 2025. The country has indicated it intends to partner with Indonesia to manage pricing & supply and impose export quotas. The two conglomerates own approximately 90% of the cobalt market.
  4. Related to the company, there is an upside to a potential US listing process. Glencore might provide an update on its H1 results and review the benefits. On a positive note, the company is also actively discussing with the Chinese authorities to review the Viterra/Bunge merger (Fig 3). If the deal closes, Glencore will receive $1 billion in cash and 33 million in Bunge shares, currently worth $2.4 billion. We believe this will likely allow the company to lift the intensity of its ongoing buyback.

Fig 2

Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Considering the company's latest guidance (Fig 5) and the production outlook (Fig 4), Glencore has a base rate EBITDA of $15 billion. Considering production curtailment and upside risks on price for cobalt and copper, we see upside pressure on the company's valuation. Applying a 3x multiple on the coal assets business with an aggregate EBITDA of $5.3 billion, our sub-segment valuation goes to $15.9 billion. We should consider that this is a highly generative cash asset. Applying an EV/EBITDA of 8x to the core EBITDA ($9.7 billion) for the metals division, our valuation reached $77.6 billion. So, the total enterprise value is set at $93.5 billion. Similar peers such as SCCO and FCX are traded between 8x and 10x EV/EBITDA multiple. CMOC is the #1 cobalt producer globally with a 35% share, while Glencore is the number #2 with a 12% share. Considering the company's current debt ($31 billion), Glencore's equity value is $62.5 billion. So, our twelve-month share price is set at £4 per share ($10 in ADR), confirming our buy rating estimates. At the time of writing, the company trades at 5x EV/EBITDA vs a median average of 7.79x (Fig 6 - which is more aligned with our blending valuation methodology by segment: metals and coal assets).

Fig 4

Fig 5

Fig 6

Risks

We have extensive coverage of Glencore downside risks as well as mining companies. So, we include 1) a slowdown in economic activities and further GDP cuts, 2) lower output prices (especially in cobalt and copper), 3) value destruction M&A, 4) ESG downside pressure, 5) regulatory risks, 6) the volatile nature of commodity prices and FX. Furthermore, the sector is exposed to political, financial, and operational risks, each of which has the potential to impact company performance significantly. We also report a faster-than-expected ramp-up in new mines.

Conclusion

Across our mining coverage with Anglo-American, Glencore has the highest upside potential. The company offers an attractive valuation and a number of potential positive near-term catalysts, so we confirmed a buy.

data.title: Tesla: A Nuanced Bull/Bear Conversation
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

A bull (The Value Edge) and bear (Pacifica Yield) discuss Tesla. Brand damage and Q1 deliveries (0:55). Are TSLA's plans for the future enough to be long-term bullish on the stock? (9:20) What data points will drive a turnaround? (17:10) How to fairly value Tesla (19:20). Tesla's competitors (26:35). Tesla ecosystem as a whole (33:35)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome to Investing Experts, Pacifica Yield and The Value Edge. We are getting into Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) (TSX:TSLA:CA) stock today. A perfect day, I think, to cover the stock given the delivery numbers they just announced and how the market is taking it.

Welcome to the show. First of all, it's great to talk to you on Seeking Alpha. Maybe let's start with a positive take given all the negativity hanging on in the world.

The Value Edge, talk to us about what you thought of Tesla's numbers today and broadly speaking, how you see Tesla.

The Value Edge: So obviously the numbers today, they missed the market expectations a bit.

We saw it dipped down quite significantly before market open, and then it was sort of a mixed reaction as the market went. I think there's been - you can't really argue - there's been some brand damage over the past few months.

Elon Musk, his involvement in politics has definitely, it's obviously been a controversial topic. And I think he disenfranchised, and I wrote on this recently, I think he disenfranchised a big portion of his existing customer base. And so you probably didn't see that in this report. You may see it in future production delivery reports.

But I think the important part is they're producing, what is it, 362,000 vehicles in Q1. You look back, these are near record numbers, if not record numbers. I mean, five years ago, this was a very small company in terms of total production. So the production rate is growing.

Obviously, in '23, we had some pretty material margin compression, but I think the unit economics are recovering again.

We're seeing that in more recent reports. So it's not great, and it definitely highlights the environment we're in with sort of weakening EV demand, a weakening auto market, maybe a weakening consumer.

But from the fundamental perspective of the company, right, you've got over the course of five years, they've probably more than doubled production. I don't know the exact metrics. I'm gonna pull it up on the site here, but they're growing their production quite significantly. They've got a global manufacturing footprint.

They've changed how the auto manufacturing process works from their gigacasting to all the automation or the actual human touch they involve in there. So I think it's still a fundamentally good story.

You've got growth, but it's just, there are some weak points, and there's quite a bit of brand damage, I think that the company is gonna be working through for quite a bit of time here. You can't really avoid that with Elon's activities and his extracurriculars.

So I don't think it's the end of the world. It obviously wasn't what the market was looking for, but, I think you look five years out, that production number is gonna continue growing.

That production number the unit economics are probably going to continue improving at least, or stay flat. But this company has a very good track record of improving their unit economics.

So I think it's sort of fine. I think you just look out a few years, and this will be the result of a weak macro environment and a somewhat weak consumer with a little bit of extracurriculars added in there.

Five years from now, it's probably a distant memory.

RS: Let me ask you before I ask Pacifica Yield to share his bearish take on the stock. We saw those disappointing numbers, but then we saw the stock rise on the day.

Are you of the opinion that that has to do with the report that came out that ostensibly Elon Musk will be taking away from his DOGE role and putting more time into his Tesla role? Are you of the opinion that that's the thing that pushed it up today, or what are your thoughts on why it's up despite disappointing numbers?

TVE: Yeah. I would say you're probably right. I would think less government involvement is a net positive, a net benefit for the stock because investors care about the business.

And if Elon's extracurriculars are hurting the brand reputation, brand value, that's going to have very real impacts on the business. We see people are going out in the real-world and damaging Teslas. They're damaging dealerships.

None of this is good for a business. So I think that takes a big bit of uncertainty out of it, and it also will help it fade out of memory over time of Elon's intense involvement in a very contentious administration. You're sort of demonizing yourself to half of your population and making a hero out of yourself out to the other half.

data.title: Asia Takes Big Tariffs Hit: Vietnam, Thailand Most Affected, While Exemptions Ease Impact For India, Singapore
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava

APAC hit by tariffs in the range of 10-49%

US President Trump announced a blanket tariff of 10% on US imports, though most APAC countries are being hit with an additional 10% or more on top of that rate. The largest tariffs seem targeted at countries exporting lower-value-added items like footwear, furniture, garments, and textiles. Vietnam, which after China has the largest trade deficit with the US, faces a 46% tariff. Cambodia got hit with a 49% tax, while Sri Lanka got 44% and Bangladesh got 37%.

The largest economies in the region, such as India, Japan, and South Korea, are relatively better off with tariffs in the 24-26% range. Additionally, their key manufacturing sectors with substantial exports to the US, like pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, benefit from tariff exemptions.

Thailand and Taiwan face relatively higher tariffs due to their larger trade surpluses with the US. In contrast, Australia and Singapore have relatively low tariffs at 10%, reflecting their smaller bilateral trade deficits with the US.

Large tariffs hit Asia

Tariffs will impact Vietnam's GDP the most, followed by Thailand

Hefty tariffs, especially on Vietnam and Thailand, pose significant growth challenges. That’s not just because of direct exposure to US imports, but also indirect hits via exports to the US through other countries. In the chart below, we stack up the value-add by each Asian country in terms of its own exports to the US (direct impact). We then add to it the value-add by each Asian country in the rest of the world’s exports to the US (indirect impact).

Vietnam and Taiwan stand out with the highest total exposure to US imports -– and with large direct exposure. However, semiconductors are exempted from tariffs, which should reduce the blow on Taiwan. Semiconductors aside, Taiwan’s export growth has been quite heavily concentrated in the US. The tariffs will threaten to dampen that growth (see link).

For Vietnam, with total exposure to US imports of 12% of GDP, a 46% tariff rate -- ceteris paribus and assuming demand elasticity of 1 -- would put 5.5% of Vietnam’s GDP at risk. Similarly, Thailand’s exposure of 9% of GDP, with a tariff rate of 36%, risks 3% of GDP being impacted in the medium term.

Vietnam and Taiwan stand out with highest exposure to US imports

Exemptions on Pharma and Semiconductors (for now) should reduce the blow for India, Singapore and Japan

Although Singapore has significant high value-added exposure to US imports, two factors could mitigate the impact: 1) a relatively low tariff rate of 10% on Singapore's direct exports and 2) pharmaceutical exports to the US,. They account for 0.3% of GDP and remain exempt from tariffs, lessening the blow. However, the most notable concern is Singapore's indirect exposure to the US, where the impact is likely to be more pronounced.

Similarly, India benefits from exemptions on pharmaceuticals. The US is India’s largest export market for pharmaceuticals, and a full-flown tariff hit would’ve been detrimental. As we discussed here: Advanced talks with the US lower the risk of high reciprocal tariffs on India. While Korea and Japan also benefit from exemptions on semiconductors and Pharma, these latter economies will still face significant challenges from the already announced 25% tariffs on automobiles and auto parts.

India and Singapore benefit from exemptions on pharmaceuticals

Vietnam and Thailand also stand out in their relatively higher exposure to US in the auto sector

Thailand is a major auto manufacturing hub in ASEAN. It’s already suffering a sharp downturn amid rising competition from Chinese electric vehicles (EVs) and weak domestic demand. This puts the sector at further risk. In terms of overall Asia exposure to US auto imports, Korea stands to lose the most with value-add exposure at 1.9% of GDP, followed by Japan at 1.3%.

Already announced tariffs on Auto sector to hit Asia as well

Room for negotiations with US, which could lower the hit to growth

Retaliatory tariffs from Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) members and India look unlikely. But uncertainty remains as countries continue to negotiate. Economies such as Vietnam made last-minute efforts to avoid tariffs. Unfortunately, they were unsuccessful in escaping sweeping tariffs. Vietnam is likely to continue negotiating with the US by making further concessions, such as lowering import duties on a range of goods, including cars, liquefied gas, and some agricultural products.

China overcapacity increases the risk of lower inflation for the region

Inflationary pressures in Asia are at their lowest in three years, with most countries seeing headline consumer price index (CPI) readings within central banks’ target range. High tariffs on China are exacerbating its industrial overcapacity, which poses a significant threat to both domestic and external demand, particularly for ASEAN countries.

China's dominance and overcapacity in clean energy sectors like EVs, solar panels, and batteries, is now extending to a wider set of industries. These include steel, petrochemicals, semiconductors, and other low-value-added sectors like footwear, furniture, garments, and textiles. It’s not only impacting ASEAN economies directly by displacing local production for some final goods, but also indirectly by displacing exports to the rest of the world. Reduced demand for goods should keep inflation well-contained in Asia.

Rapid acceleration in ASEAN’s imports from China

Risk of faster monetary policy easing as growth concerns deepen

Downside risks to growth, low inflation, and pressures coming from China's manufacturing overcapacity risk displacing ASEAN exports. All this suggests further monetary policy easing in Asia is coming. We already have additional rate cuts in our 2025 forecast profile for Korea (75bps) Indonesia (50bps), Philippines (75bps), India (75bps), Singapore (slope reduction), and Australia (75bps). However, tariff war escalation could mean that the rate cuts come in faster than our expectations. The aim would be to proactively ward off downward growth pressures. One country where the scope for rate cuts might increase is Thailand, where headline CPI is likely to slip into negative territory in the second quarter. The relatively large impact tariffs have on growth could further shift the central bank's stance in favour of rate cuts.

Asia inflation likely to fall further, monetary easing to continue

Implications for FX

Asian currencies generally appreciated modestly against the USD in the first quarter of 2025, except for the HKD, TWD, and IDR. This was largely influenced by the Europe re-rating story. Consequently, we believe the market underestimated the tariff risks, which are proving worse than anticipated. For the CNY, the latest round of tariffs will likely add to downward pressures in the near term. But we don’t expect significant devaluation pressures on the CNY, as the People’s Bank of China (PBoC) remains firm on its currency stability objective (see here for details).

Asian currencies may face increased depreciation pressure as markets turn risk-averse, affecting foreign institutional investor (FII) inflows. Delayed investment decisions could impact foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows into the region, while exporters may devalue their currencies to stay price-competitive

More specifically, the tariff announcements have further reinforced our view for a weaker Thai Baht vs. USD, one based on weaker external and fiscal balances and a cautious view on growth. Vietnamese Dong (VND) fell to an all-time low of 25803 vs the USD and the upside pressure on USD/VND is likely to continue. INR, PHP and SGD are likely to outperform the region in the near-term given the relatively lesser blow from tariffs, while KRW should continue to underperform in second quarter of 2015, when sentiment will likely be at its worst (see here for details)
